expected_findings.detail: A prior year state refund is only taxable to the extent it produced a federal tax benefit (the §111 tax benefit rule). If prior year was at the SALT cap, the refund is usually NOT taxable. Do the worksheet.

expected_findings.summary: Harper has up to $7,000 of TY2025 IRA headroom
expected_findings.detail: Harper is 44 years old; the TY2025 IRA contribution limit is $7,000. Deductibility may phase out at high AGI for traditional IRA if covered by an employer plan; Roth IRA phases out at $236K–$246K MFJ.

expected_findings.summary: AGI above Roth IRA phaseout — consider backdoor Roth
expected_findings.detail: AGI $217,400 is above the single Roth IRA phaseout ceiling ($165,000). Direct Roth contributions are not allowed. Consider the backdoor Roth strategy (nondeductible traditional IRA contribution + Roth conversion) if there is no pre-tax IRA balance to trigger pro-rata.
Harper has contributed $14,000 toward the TY2025 §402(g) limit of $23,500. Another $9,500 can still be deferred before year end.

expected_findings.summary: Underpayment: need $35,475 withheld for 110% safe harbor
expected_findings.detail: Prior year AGI exceeded $150,000, so the §6654 safe harbor requires 110% of prior year total tax ($35,475) to be paid via withholding or quarterly estimates. Currently $25,800 is withheld; consider a Q4 estimated payment or increased withholding of $9,675 to avoid the §6654 penalty.

expected_findings.summary: Need $35,219 withheld to hit 90% current year safe harbor
expected_findings.detail: The alternative safe harbor is 90% of current year tax. This is usually only easier to hit than 110% prior year if income dropped.

Based on a shortfall of $9,419 and the TY2025 applicable interest rate of ~8%, the underpayment penalty is approximately $377. Consider the annualized income method (Schedule AI) if income was lumpy across quarters.
